questions.q: Why does a computer system require multiple levels of memory (registers, cache, RAM, disk) instead of a single unified memory?
options: Different levels serve different purposes; registers are fast but small, disk is large but slow, and caches bridge the gap to match processor speed expectations
Only embedded systems use hierarchies; modern computers can use a single unified memory
Multiple levels allow the processor to ignore memory access latency entirely
Disk and RAM serve identical functions and are redundant
questions.explanation: Memory hierarchy exploits the speed/size trade-off: fast memory is expensive and small (registers, cache), while large memory is slow (RAM, disk). Caching bridges the gap, presenting the capacity of slow memory with near-speed of fast memory. Single unified memory cannot exist; different levels have distinct purposes.

questions.q: A register stores a value used by the current instruction and can be accessed in 0 cycles (no latency). Why can't main memory operate at register speed?
options: Registers are made of superior materials that main memory cannot use
Physical distance, capacitance, and the need to drive long wires make main memory inherently slower
Main memory is intentionally designed to be slow for cost reasons
Processors cannot address registers beyond a few hundred
questions.explanation: Registers are on-die with minimal wire distance and switching overhead. Main memory requires long wires off-chip, charge distribution across large arrays, and sense amplifiers, all causing multi-cycle latencies. Cost is secondary; the physics of large memory arrays dictates speed.

questions.q: A processor has a 10 GHz clock (0.1 ns per cycle) and main memory latency of 100 ns. How many processor cycles elapse during a single main memory access?
options: 10 cycles
100 cycles
1000 cycles
0.1 cycles
questions.explanation: Number of cycles = memory latency / clock period = 100 ns / 0.1 ns = 1000 cycles. This vast latency gap illustrates why caches are essential; without them, the processor would stall for most program execution.

questions.q: Virtual memory maps a program's logical address space to physical memory or disk. What is a primary benefit of virtual memory?
options: Virtual memory eliminates the need for paging by fitting all programs in physical RAM
Virtual memory allows each program to use a private address space larger than physical memory and protects programs from each other
Virtual memory replaces caching and makes hierarchical memory unnecessary
Virtual memory can only be used on systems with disk storage
questions.explanation: Virtual memory provides protection and allows programs to address more memory than physically available (using disk as overflow). Each program sees its own private address space starting at 0. Virtual memory and caching serve different purposes and coexist.

questions.q: When a virtual memory page is not in physical RAM, a page fault occurs and the page must be fetched from disk. Why is the page fault overhead acceptable despite disk latency being in milliseconds?
options: Page faults happen extremely frequently and are unavoidable
Page faults are rare; they only occur during context switches
Once a page is loaded into RAM, subsequent accesses are fast; the high cost is amortized over many accesses to that page
Disks are actually faster than main memory
questions.explanation: Page faults are rare in well-designed systems due to locality; once a page is loaded, many accesses occur before the next fault. The cost is amortized over these accesses, making average access time much lower than the fault latency.

questions.q: In a system with 4 KB pages and a physical memory of 16 GB, how many distinct physical page frames exist?
options: 1 million
4 million
256 million
questions.explanation: Number of page frames = physical memory size / page size = 16 GB / 4 KB = 16,000,000,000 / 4,096 ≈ 4 million frames. Each frame can hold one page.

questions.q: A TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) is a small cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations. What problem does the TLB solve?
options: The TLB speeds up virtual address translation by caching page table lookups, avoiding slow memory accesses for every address translation
The TLB provides write-through caching for all memory accesses
The TLB eliminates page faults by predicting future memory accesses
The TLB prevents multiple programs from running simultaneously
questions.explanation: Every virtual address requires a page table lookup, which is a memory access and much slower than register access. The TLB caches these translations, dramatically reducing translation latency. The TLB does not eliminate faults or prevent concurrency.

questions.q: In a memory hierarchy, bandwidth refers to the amount of data transferred per unit time. Why does memory bandwidth decrease at deeper hierarchy levels (from registers to disk)?
options: Deeper levels are intentionally designed with lower bandwidth to conserve power
The architecture of large memories (disk, RAM) involves serial access patterns and contention among many requests, limiting concurrent transfers
Bandwidth always increases at deeper levels to compensate for latency
Bandwidth is independent of hierarchy level
questions.explanation: Registers are single-purpose with dedicated buses; caches use wide parallel buses; RAM involves fewer banks but memory contention; disks access data sequentially from rotating platters. The larger the memory, the harder it is to provide parallel access, reducing bandwidth.

questions.q: A processor uses a write-through cache for L1 and a write-back cache for L2. When the L1 cache writes through to L2, both caches are updated. However, if L2 uses write-back, when are the updates propagated to main memory?
options: Immediately after the L1 write
When the L2 line is evicted and marked dirty
After every L1 write-through regardless of L2 state
Write-back does not propagate updates
questions.explanation: L2 write-back delays updates to memory until the L2 line is evicted. L1 write-through updates L2 immediately, but L2 still batches memory writes using its write-back policy. This hybrid approach balances consistency and bandwidth.

questions.q: A program has high temporal locality but poor spatial locality. Which memory hierarchy level will have the lowest miss rate for this workload?
options: Registers will have minimal misses because of high temporal locality, but cache and main memory will suffer due to poor spatial locality
The memory hierarchy will fail to improve performance for this pattern
Spatial locality is always more important than temporal locality
This pattern guarantees zero cache misses at all levels
questions.explanation: High temporal locality means the same data is accessed repeatedly, allowing reuse within cache lines and reducing misses. Poor spatial locality means the CPU is not reusing the entire cached block, wasting capacity. Registers and small caches exploit temporal reuse; larger caches waste space on unused block contents.
